We need some basic infrastructure to validate ideas, especially in the early stages when we're making our first few turns of the TDB flywheel. At the most basic level, we need a way to generate awareness for our ideas, some kind of landing page container that expresses those ideas, a next action that creates a signal, for example, opt-in, scheduled call, sale, etc., and tools to measure performance. We'll use Facebook ads to generate awareness, that's the subject to validate, part four. However, before we do that, we'll need to set up the minimum viable infrastructure required to get the feedback we need to iterate our way to success. Broadly speaking, there are two options we can implement. Option one is to use a hosted solution that meets some or all of our initial needs. Those options include landing page builders like led pages and unbounce, or more robust solutions like click funnels, that also includes email, e-commerce, and content creation, sharing capabilities. Option two is to build a solution using more robust tools like WordPress, integrating other specific solutions like email service providers, convert kit, for example, and commerce, click bank, for example. There is no right answer here, each has positives and negatives. In general, option one is faster in the short term. It's very easy to set up a lead gen page in unbounce, for example, or an opt-in page with email follow-up and click funnels. However, one significant danger with option one is the opportunity cost associated with spinning up temporary solutions that have to be recreated later as your business matures. That leads us to the primary benefit of option two, which is it's a more durable solution. There's more work to set up in the beginning but less work overall because you won't have to migrate content, leads, etc. Another potential issue with option one is that the temporary solution becomes the long-term solution. It's difficult to break free from the inertia of a working solution, and often, that's most difficult when your business is growing rapidly. We'll explain the options in more detail below and share the approach we'll be taking. Please remember that whatever option you choose should be in service to the larger goal of validating your initial ideas, and whatever you create should be the minimum necessary to accomplish that task. There will be many opportunities to expand and improve your infrastructure over time in future turns of the TDB flywheel. In the startup world, the term MVP, minimum viable product, is used to represent a minimum version of an idea or product to collect the maximum amount of validated learning about customers with the least risk, as measured by time, energy, and dollars. However, we're not there yet. We're still seeking to validate, iterate towards, a working offer, a marketing expression of a product or service that has a message to market match. For this reason, we prefer to use a different term to describe this part of the process. To this end, a minimum viable experiment, MVE, more closely expresses our intent. An MVE will inform a downstream MVP, our release 1.0 product. In this lesson, we'll build our MVE environment. Infrastructure, landing page, website. This section can be tricky because it has the potential to open Pandora's box. This can be fertile ground to procrastinate. Don't do that. The goal is to build whatever is minimally necessary to get your initial ideas out into the world and get feedback from your prospective audience. To do that, you can use Office Shelf Page or Site Builders, option 1 mentioned above, or set up a more robust site development environment and then build only the infrastructure you need for your experiments, option 2. Let's start by looking at option 1, Page, Site Builders. If you're already paying for one, or more, posted page or site building solutions, we recommended that you use those to minimize friction to get started. Pricing can vary from a few bucks, Wix, to hundreds of dollars each month, depending on your needs. Some popular solutions include Unbounce, Let Pages, Wix, InstaPage, ClickFunnels, HubSpot, Simplero. If already used ConvertKit, it comes with a free landing page builder. The second option is a more robust website builder, like WordPress. If you don't already use a landing page or site building service, our suggestion, broadly speaking, is to consider what you'll likely use long term for your business. We're not a fan building a business on someone else's platform, for example, ClickFunnels. Although it's relatively quick to get set up if money isn't a constraint, there are many less obvious constraints downstream if you were to run your business on a marketing platform like ClickFunnels or HubSpot. We prefer owning our own platform, so we're not beholden to a gatekeeper or subject to the limitations of the platform we're using, for example, SiteSpeed issues mentioned below. That said, it's easy to get caught up researching the perfect self-hosted tech stack and then never make it out the gate. For this reason, we're going to give you our lean self-hosted stack, with the minimum number of options to get started. This is the exact stack we use ourselves. From there, you can orient yourself to options that better match your needs and budget. We use WordPress because it's durable, extensible, and it powers a quarter of the world's websites. There's an enormous developer community for WordPress and a rich ecosystem of add-ons and integrations that make it easier to use and more powerful. Our self-hosted lean tech stack includes WordPress, Direct Download, pre-installed on our recommended hosting platforms. Hosting, we use Kinsta and WP Engine because they're reliable and fast. Kinsta starts at $30 per month and WP at $25 per month and both are best in Class 1. Premium themes, thesis slash focus or generate press. Thesis slash focus, $87 per year for a single site license, or $187 per year for five sites too. That's less than $40 per month for a best in Class self-hosted solution. One of the nice things with both Kinsta and WP Engine is that deploying a site is one click easy, WP deployed, optimized, with SSL slash HTTPS included. If you want to go big, five sites, you're looking at around $115 per month. That's incredible value. One note about hosting, we can say without a shred of hesitation, having cumulatively been paying for hosting for near enough 40 years, that you get what you pay for. Yes, you can find unlimited website hosting for five bucks a month, but we caution against that. It's one thing hosting a personal blog, yes, cheap hosting will do just fine, but orders of magnitude different when speed and uptime matter. When you're paying for traffic, the last thing you want to discover hours later is that your host was down or sluggish when it mattered most. In stark contrast, both Kinsta and WP run on Google Cloud Platform, a globally distributed network of data centers, running C2 virtual machines, each with up to 60 CPUs and hundreds of gigabytes of RAM. Put another way, TLB runs on the same servers and hardware stack as Google.com, and for as little as $30 per month. It never goes down, and it's always ridiculously fast. Two note about WordPress themes, it's tempting to seek out a free WordPress theme that can build decent looking landing pages to keep costs low. It's even more tempting to use a page builder like DV or Elementor, to essentially have your own self-hosted version of ClickFunnels or Leadpages, where building landing pages is drag and drop easy. This is a decision only you can make, but we would be doing you a disservice if we didn't draw attention to the alternative. Then, whatever decision you make is an informed one. DV and Elementor on WordPress are not lightweight affairs, the same can be said for hosted solutions like ClickFunnels and Leadpages. Technically speaking, they're dogs. The HTML code they produce to create landing pages is bloated, 1000s of lines of code when 100s would do. The homepage of ClickFunnels is 3437 lines long, the rendered version is not a long page. The homepage of Buffer.com, rendered version looks similar in length to CF, is 356 lines. That's 10x smaller, which means it can load that much faster. Speed matters. This is why we've been using Thesis Slash Focus for well over a decade, Focus is a new skin that runs on top of the Thesis framework. It's, very, lightweight, which means it's fast. Thesis Slash Focus is what we use on TLB, and the demo site we built for you. If we couldn't use Thesis Slash Focus for whatever reason, GeneratePress would be our second choice. It's also lightweight and produces well optimized pages. Unlike with Thesis Slash Focus, GP has a free version, and the premium version, which unlocks the good stuff, runs at just $59 per year for a license. Our recommendation, if it's not already obvious, if you decide to go with a self-hosted WordPress platform, use Thesis Slash Focus, our number one choice, or GeneratePress. That's it. To help you visualize what a landing page can look like, we've built a demo version you can use to inspire your own. It's not meant to be a template for you to copy. We're not going to give you our custom CSS. This is intended to help give you an idea of how we build landing pages. There are a million variations, none better than the next within a given context. We'll keep tweaking and adding to this demo site, we'll create different scenarios within it throughout the training. Please don't link to the site from your blog, if you have one, or the socials. It's exclusively for TDB students. We would appreciate it if it remained within the family, so to speak. Infrastructure, tracking. To measure results, we suggest that you set up and install. Google Tag Manager, optional, but highly recommended. Google Analytics. Facebook Pixel. If you choose option two, there are third-party tools to easily deploy Google Tag Manager in a WordPress environment. A quick YouTube or Google search will show you how to install GTM manually as well. If you're using a page or site building tool, consult that specific tools to support documentation. Some tools don't work well with Google Tag Manager, for example, and most have a custom method for installing code appropriately. A quick Google search should reveal details for whatever platforms you're choosing. Make sure your tracking tools are set up and tested before you start your first experiment. You don't want to find out tracking was set up incorrectly after the fact. Finally, here's the high-level workflow you'll want to follow to prepare to run your first experiment. Validate, Part 2, Messaging and Writing Framework. Validate, Part 3, Testing Framework. Build, Part I, Landing Page Framework. Validate, Part 4, FB Testing Traffic Framework. Ask questions in the comments below and discuss in the community. Andrei and Sean.
